attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted to hospital in active labour at 39 weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy which has been uncomplicated. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. The cervix is 5 cm dilated. Cardiotocography (CTG) monitoring shows a baseline fetal heart rate of 140/min with good variability. During contractions, there are irregular decreases in fetal heart rate of 30-60 beats/min, varying in timing and depth, with each showing a rapid onset and recovery within 30 seconds.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Decelerations that vary in shape, timing, and depth from contraction to contraction, with rapid onset and recovery are characteristic of variable decelerations. These are often caused by umbilical cord compression. Variable decelerations are the most common type of deceleration pattern seen during labour.

Early decelerations are caused by fetal head compression during contractions. They have a consistent shape and timing, beginning and ending with the contraction, creating a mirror image of the contraction pattern. They are considered physiological and do not indicate fetal compromise.

Late decelerations begin after the peak of the contraction and return to baseline after the contraction ends. They have a gradual onset and recovery and are consistent in their relationship to contractions. Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency and are associated with fetal hypoxia.

Fetal bradycardia is a deceleration lasting greater than 5 minutes. They typically have a clear precipitating event such as epidural insertion, uterine hyperstimulation, or maternal hypotension.

Sinusoidal patterns are characterized by a smooth, sine wave-like undulating pattern without normal beat-to-beat variability. This is a rare but ominous pattern associated with severe fetal anaemia or hypoxia.

The management of variable decelerations depends on their frequency and severity. Mild and occasional variable decelerations are common and may not require intervention beyond continued monitoring. However, frequent or deep variable decelerations may require position changes, reducing contraction frequency, or consideration of expedited delivery if they persist or worsen.
learningPoints: Variable decelerations are characterized by an abrupt decrease in fetal heart rate that varies in timing and shape from contraction to contraction.
Variable decelerations are caused by umbilical cord compression and are the most common type of deceleration in labour.
Early and late decelerations have a consistent relationship with contractions, unlike variable decelerations which are irregular.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Intrapartum fetal surveillance. East Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021 [cited 2024 Nov 5]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
